Gentlemen:
I presently
Calistoga, California. serve as eity Attorney for the City of
A situation has recently arisen in Calistoga
concerning which r want to seek your advice and direition.
Ehe city has been interested for some tine in acguiring land.
for irrigation with treated waste water. Land for other
nunicipal purposes has also been under consideration.
Late last year, a conmittee was appointed,
consisting of members of the council and stltr. 
At the time
the committee was serected and charged with its work, it was
known that only a few parcels cf vacant land existed within
the city which might be appricabre. one of those sites was
owned by a present member of calistogars city council. That
member of the council did not participate in-the decision to
appoint a committee or in its serection or functioning.
The committee has now made its report to the
council and has reconmended that the city I-ook into the
acguisition of the councir nemberrs property. Although r was
not city Attorney last fall when the- cornnitlee was first
appointed, Ty preaecessor warned the council of the many
problems and difficurties arising from the possible
acguisition of property from the council member. she
specifically advised then of.the problems posed by statutes,
such as Government code section rbgo. she also pioria.a them

with coples and a brief analysis of the California Suprene

I wrote a memoranduro to the council accompanying
the conmitteere report. I enclose a copy of that mernorandun
for your review. The councilnan who owns the property,
Robert l{axf ield, has acted preciseLy in accordance rrith ny
recommendatlons.
l,Iy guestions to the Fair Political Practices
ConmiEsion are: (1) whether there are regulations,
guidelines or other prlnted naterial available to guide a
nunlcipality through the possible acquisition of property
fron a nenber of the city council; and (2) whether Comrnissj-on
staff has any comnents or additional input to supplernent the
materLal contained in ny written nenorandum.
Unless I receive some other infonnation from you,
my reading of the applicable statutory and case Law would
lead ne to reconmend to the council that they take the
statutory steps leading toward the institution of a
condennation action. After that action is fited, I lrould
then seek an early status conference lrlth our 1ocal Superior
Court in which I rrould Eeek to have the Court appoint a
special naster to oversee negotiations leadinq to the Cityts
acquisition of the Uaxfield parcel . I lrould direct that in
the meantime, the reconmendations contained in ny letter
renain in effect.
Thank you for whatever inforrnation or assistance
you can give ne. I will look forward to hearing fron you.

site selection for Treated waste water and connunitv Needs
fntroduction:
The Site selection committee regardj.ng treated t aste water
and conmunity needs has nade a recornmendation to the litayor and
Council, dated January 15, 1991. That reconmendation indicates
that the City should consider acquisition of the Robert Maxfield
property.
That recoumendat,ion raises a nuBber of legal problems,
because of Robert, Maxfield's status as a roelDber of the city
council . This Memorandum wil]. discuss those questions, make
recoMendations to the Council as to hot, to proceed, and discuss
the legal reasoning for the recoEDendations.
ouestions Raised:
The questions raised by the site Selection coEmittee's
recoumendations include:
(1) uay the City Council discuss and act upon the
Coumittee' s reconuendati.ons?
(2') What restrictions and gruidelines apply in any
discussion and action by the council?
Reconmendations:
For the reasons that are explored later in this
MeDorandum, I recoEDend that the City Council act as follows on
the Site Selectj-on coEmittee, s report:
(1) The Council nay discuss and act upon the CoEmittee, s
recoEmendation.
(21 council rneuber Robert tltaxfield ray not participate in
the discussion, and it would be desirable if he was not phylically
present in the council chaaber while the discussion took-place.
-1-
(


(3) council lnellber Roberc t{axfield should not discuss the
coEnittee's recoMendations with other council nenbers or with
staff.
(4) If the council lrere to determine that acquisj.tion of
the Maxfield parcel was desirable, neither the Council nor city
staff should undertake any negotiation vith Robert lttaxfield
relating to the acquisition of the property.
(5) If the council were to deternine that acquisition of
the tilaxfield parcel was desi.rable, acquisition should only take
place through the eDinent domain process.
(6) The council should direct its 1ega1 and
administrative representatives to seek guidance from the
California Fair Political Practices Conmission as to tbe
appropriate trethods of proceedj.ng.
Di scuss ion :
The problens raised by the acquisition of a council
Dern.ber's property by a city arise principally as a result of
GoveraEent code Section 1090. That section reads, in pertinent
part, as follows:
nllenbers of the Legislature, state, county,
district, judicial district, and citY officers
or eoployees shall not be financially interested
in any contract ruade by thern in their official
capacity, or by any body or board of which they
are roeubers. Nor shal1 state, county, district,
judicial district, and city offj.cers or
euployees be purchasers at any sale or vendors
at any purchase Eade by then in their official
caPacity. tr
Section 1090 is a very direct and straightforward ru].e
rrhich sinply prohibits a Council neuber fron any financial
involveroent with a city. 
The section has been in effect for uany
years and Dany court cases have interpreted its very strict
requirements. As courts have often pointed out, even a contract
which is fair to tbe city is unlawful, and the fact that fu1l
disclosure is made is irrelevant; even abstentj-on by the nenber
does not cure the probleB. As the california Supreme court
indicated in its most recent case construing Secti.on 1090, r.Uere
nenbership on the board or council establishes the presunption
that tbe officer part,j-cipated in tbe forbidden transactj.on or
influenced otber menbers of the council .ri (Thomson vs. call
(1985), 38 Cal.3d 633, 649, 214 CaI.Rptr. 139)
the li:nitations on the city,s actions are not on].y strict,
but the penalties for noncompliance are quite onerous. Ihe nost
lecent California Supreue Court case which construes Section 1o9O
-2-


(Thomson v. Ca1I, supra) involved the acquisition by the City of
Albany of some land belonging to a councj.l EeEber. The
acguisition r,ras somewhat convoluted and indirect, but the trial
court deternined that it was part of an overalL transaction
lthereby the city acquired the land from a neEber of the city
councj-l . The counciluan j-n question did not participate in most
aspects of the transaction, and roade f,u1l discLosure of his
ownership of the property. The council. menber sought, received
and followed the advice of the city's attorney in his actions
relating to the transaction.
In spite of all of these factors and in spite of an
apparent absence of fraud and what the court called a possible
rgood faithtr tristake on the council uenber's parE, the court
upheld the iuposition of a harsh penalty upon the councj.l neuber.
That penalty rras the forfeiture of the entire purchase price
received by hin i.n the transaction.
The case which I have just described underscores the
difficulty 
of a city acquiring property belongi.ng to a council
menber. Such acquisj.tion remains possible, holrever. Sone hints
as to how to proceed are contained in a 1988 California Court of
Appeal deci.sion (Santa clara va11ev water District v. cross
(1988), 200 Ca1.Ap.3d 1353, 245 Cal.Rptr. 580). In that case, a
lrater district 
sought to acquire property belongi.ng to a nenber of
its board of directors, and the specific issue before the court
involved whether certain rules in eninent donaj.n litigation
applied j.n light of Govern:!.ent code Section 1090. The court held
that the rufes did apply, and seeroed to suggest that eihen an
eni.nent douain action is filed, that soue of the requirements of,
Section 1090 Bay not apply. Trro caveats should be noted, however:
first, 
it j.s clear that council roember Maxfield should not
negotiate with other nenbers of the Council or staff; and, second,
the FPPC nay not view the matter in the same nanner as the Court
of Appeal.
It is for this reason that I advocate seeking the FPPc's
advice.
conclusion.
If the Council deteroi.nes that acquisition should be
further explored, steps toward euinent douain should be
undereaken, and the FPPC,S advj.ce shoul,d be sought.
